Paul cuts through the Corinthian church's obsession with status and wisdom by holding up a mirror: these are stewards of God's mysteries, not celebrities. The word "steward" is devastatingly practical—a servant who manages what belongs to someone else. A steward is accountable, not autonomous. He answers to his master, not to the applause of onlookers. The Corinthians have gotten this backward entirely. They are comparing Paul and Apollos, taking pride in human teachers, boasting as though they had generated their own spiritual gifts. But Paul's question pierces all that noise: "What do you have that you did not receive?" The answer collapses their entire framework. Everything—your wisdom, your gifts, your standing in Christ—came to you as a gift. To boast in it is to claim credit for something that was given to you. This isn't spiritual maturity; it's spiritual amnesia about your own poverty before God.

The contrast Paul then draws is almost unbearable. The Corinthians feel satisfied, rich, and kingly. Meanwhile, Paul and the other apostles are hungry, beaten, homeless, slandered—treated as "the scum of the earth, the refuse of the world." This is not poetry or exaggeration. This is what apostolic ministry looks like. And Paul doesn't present this as tragedy to be pitied but as the actual shape of faithfulness to Christ. He became a spectacle, displayed at the end of a procession like a prisoner condemned to death. Yet he calls this following Christ, and he calls the Corinthians to imitate not their comfortable status but his suffering. This demand is relentless: if you want to follow Jesus, look at what that cost the apostle who brought you the gospel.

But Paul is not being cruel to shame them. He is a father warning his children. He has sent Timothy to remind them what genuine Christian life looks like—not the appearance of success and honor, but the reality of sacrifice and dependence on God's power. Some among them have grown arrogant, apparently assuming Paul won't actually return. Paul promises he will come, and when he does, he will see what these arrogant people are truly made of. The question he leaves hanging is urgent: which will it be? Correction with the rod, or love and gentleness? Paul is demonstrating what true authority looks like—not the authority to make yourself comfortable and admired, but the authority to shepherd a flock, sometimes through necessary hardship. The kingdom of God does not consist in impressive talk but in the actual power of God working through weakness, suffering, and obedience. That is what a steward serves. That is what demands his faithfulness.
